title: Mitigating Object Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models via Image-Grounded Guidance

reviews.summary: The paper proposes MARINE, a training-free and API-free framework to mitigate object hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) at inference time. MARINE extracts object-level, image-grounded signals from open-source vision models (e.g., DETR, RAM++) and injects them as textual guidance, combining conditional and unconditional LVLM logits via a classifier-free guidance style interpolation controlled by a scalar γ. Across five popular LVLMs and multiple benchmarks (CHAIR, POPE, GPT-4V-aided evaluation), MARINE reduces hallucinations while largely preserving or improving overall text quality, and it achieves a favorable latency–accuracy trade-off.
strengths: The paper adapts classifier-free guidance ideas to LVLM decoding by conditioning on auxiliary image-grounded prompts and linearly combining conditional and unconditional logits per token.
The design to ensemble multiple open-source vision models and aggregate their outputs (with an intersection strategy) is simple yet effective for robust image-grounded guidance.
The method is training-free and API-free, making it broadly applicable and easy to deploy across LVLMs without architectural changes.

Extensive evaluations on five LVLMs (LLaVA, LLaVA-v1.5, MiniGPT-v2, mPLUG-Owl2, InstructBLIP) and multiple datasets (MSCOCO, A-OKVQA, GQA) using standard hallucination metrics (CHAIR, POPE) and a GPT-4V-aided judge.
Useful ablations: single vs multi guidance models; intersection vs union aggregation; and sensitivity to guidance strength γ.
Inclusion of a “MARINE-Truth” oracle variant provides an informative upper bound on potential performance.
The overall framework is well-motivated with a clear pipeline description and algorithmic pseudocode.
The role of γ and the conditional/unconditional branches is explained with intuition, and ablation curves aid understanding.
Prompts and implementation choices are documented in the appendix to assist reproducibility.
Addresses a widely recognized and practically important issue—object hallucination—without retraining.
Demonstrates consistent hallucination reductions with minimal overhead relative to competing decoding-time approaches.
The approach is model-agnostic and flexibly integrates improving external detectors/tags, suggesting lasting utility.
weaknesses: The theoretical grounding is light: while the analogy to classifier-free guidance is sensible, the derivation for autoregressive LVLMs remains heuristic, and some equations appear to contain typographical inconsistencies.

Reliance on external detectors/tags can lead to under-detection; the conservative intersection heuristic may bias toward omission, potentially impairing recall or instruction-following in some settings.
The guidance only targets object nouns; other hallucination types (attributes, spatial relations, counting) are not explicitly addressed.

Some recent, relevant, training-free or logit-steering baselines (e.g., Contrastive Region Guidance; recent “paying more attention to image” or latent-space steering methods) are cited but not compared experimentally.
The POPE “yes” ratio becomes notably conservative for some models (e.g., down to ~39%), suggesting possible overcorrection and residual bias toward “no.”
Limited sensitivity analysis of detection thresholds and aggregator behavior; the fixed high DETR threshold (0.95) may systematically suppress valid objects, and threshold sweeps are not reported.
The GPT-4V-aided evaluation shows relatively modest gains (with overlapping error bars in some cases), and statistical significance is not systematically analyzed across CHAIR/POPE.
Minor formula inconsistencies (e.g., product expansions and conditionals) can confuse the guidance interpretation; a tighter, error-free derivation would help.
Details of the aggregation module (LLM-based vs rule-based) are not fully specified in the main text; which option was used in core experiments and any cost/latency implications should be clearer.
Empirical comparisons against HALC (2024), Contrastive Region Guidance (2024), and other recent training-free steering methods are missing, limiting placement versus current state of the art.
Additional faithfulness metrics (e.g., TIFA, NOPE, ALOHa) would strengthen the claim of broader generalization beyond CHAIR/POPE.
reviews.questions:
1. What specific aggregation strategy was used in the main experiments (rule-based vs small LLM), and what was its computational cost relative to LVLM decoding? Could you share examples where aggregation changed the guidance set materially?
2. How sensitive are the results to the detector thresholds (DETR 0.95, RAM++ 0.68)? Can you provide a threshold sweep to show precision/recall trade-offs in guidance and downstream CHAIR/POPE impacts?
3. Have you tried dynamic γ schedules (e.g., higher at noun tokens or early tokens, lower otherwise) or confidence-weighted logit mixing based on guidance confidence? Could this reduce the “safe-no” bias observed in POPE for some models?
4. How does MARINE perform in domains where detector taxonomies are mismatched (e.g., medical, diagrams) or on tasks emphasizing attributes/relations (e.g., counting, spatial reasoning)? Any preliminary analyses or failure cases?
5. Could you compare against HALC and Contrastive Region Guidance, and possibly “pay more attention to image” or latent-steering methods, to more thoroughly situate MARINE among recent training-free techniques?
6. In the GPT-4V-aided evaluation, several improvements appear small and with overlapping standard deviations. Did you run multiple randomized trials and conduct statistical significance tests for CHAIR/POPE as well?
reviews.assessment: This paper addresses an important and active problem—object hallucination in LVLMs—with a practical, training-free, and API-free solution that is simple to implement and broadly applicable. The key insight—leveraging reliable image-grounded object signals and integrating them via classifier-free-style logit interpolation—achieves consistent gains across five LVLMs on CHAIR and POPE, while maintaining overall caption/VQA quality and keeping inference overhead near 2×. The ablations are thoughtful and reinforce design choices (multi-model intersection, γ control). The main weaknesses are incomplete comparisons with several recent, closely related training-free methods, limited analysis of sensitivity to detector thresholds, occasional over-conservatism (low “yes” ratios), and minor clarity issues in derivations. Overall, the contributions are solid and practically relevant; with stronger baselines and additional analyses, this work would be a compelling addition to ICML. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on addressing missing comparisons and clarifying sensitivity/aggregation details.
